figures.gloss: Demigod who snares the sun to slow the day, fishes whole islands from the sea, and steals fire for people.

Son of Rangi and Papa, atua of the ocean and the father of fish and reptiles. When the storm-brother Tāwhirimātea attacks the children who parted their parents, Tangaroa flees into the deep; his descendants split, the fish staying in the sea and the lizards taking refuge inland — a primal quarrel that, Māori say, set sea against land forever.
figures.facet: The deep here is not a slain chaos but an aggrieved BROTHER who retreats: the sea-god withdraws into his own element under the storm's assault, and the rift between his ocean-children and his land-children is read as the standing enmity of water and shore.
figures.provenance: Grey, 'Polynesian Mythology' (the children of Rangi and Papa); Best. Across wider Polynesia (Tahiti, Hawai‘i as Kanaloa) Tangaroa is elevated far higher — in Māoridom he is one brother among the storm-scattered six.

edges.note: Both are trickster culture-heroes who wrest boons from the gods by guile; Maui fishes up islands, Anansi buys the stories.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Apex divine powers of the ocean, governing the treacherous domain of seafarers and marine predators.
Both are primordial wonder-working culture-heroes who reshape the world by cunning and craft — Väinämöinen by song, Māui by trickery and the magic hook; structural kin, no contact.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A legendary culture hero whose specific actions irrevocably cement mortality for all future generations.

Kanaloa < Proto-Polynesian *Taŋaroa by exact reflex — *t > k, *ŋ > n, *r > l — the same ocean-god as Māori Tangaroa / Samoan Tagaloa; cognate of the deep, with the Hawaiian peculiarity that he is Kāne's companion rather than a paramount sky-source.

One and the same hero by descent: Proto-Polynesian *Maaui, the demigod who fishes up islands, snares the sun, and steals fire, shared across Polynesia with extraordinary fidelity — the Hawaiian and the wider-Polynesian Māui are the identical figure.

Two trickster culture-heroes who teach humankind, fight monsters, and reshape the world by audacious cunning — the Khoekhoe ancestor and the Polynesian demigod both die in the end, but Heitsi-Eibib keeps rising where Maui is finally killed.
Culture-hero tricksters whose gifts to humankind come wrapped in mischief and overreach — Iktómi the namer, Māui the fire-snarer and sun-snarer; benefaction inseparable from the prank.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The quintessential Oceanic trickster-hero who battles his brothers and wrests vital elements (like night/day) from ancient powers for humanity's benefit.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are culturally beloved, semi-divine trickster-heroes who rely on cunning rather than raw power to steal boons for humanity and alter the geography.
Both are trickster culture-heroes who master the sun — Maui snares and slows it, Raven steals and releases it. Parallel of the trickster bending the solar order to human use.

The storm drives Tangaroa into the deep; in the flight his sea-children and land-children split, setting ocean against shore — the Māori etiology of why the lizard fled inland and the fish stayed in the sea.

Why death stands: Māui crawls into the sleeping Hine-nui-te-pō to win immortality for humankind, but she wakes and crushes him in the obsidian teeth between her thighs — the trickster's one defeat, and the reason death has never been undone.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rapa Nui theology merged the ancient Polynesian sea god Tangaroa into the new, localized supreme figure of Makemake.
Both are trickster culture-heroes who reshape the cosmos by audacious acts and who give humankind its conditions of life; |Kaggen makes the moon as Maui hauls up lands and snares the sun.

The Māui cycle is written into descent and landscape alike: iwi traditions recite lines from Māui himself, and the North Island is Te Ika-a-Māui, the fish he hauled up, the South Island his canoe — the trickster fixed as ancestor and geography.

dossier.essence: The traditional religions of the Polynesian peoples of the central and eastern Pacific — Māori, Hawaiian, Tahitian, Samoan, Tongan, and others — descended from a common Ancestral Polynesian religion that formed in the Tonga-Samoa homeland (c. 1000–500 BCE) and diversified across the islands. They share a pantheon of atua (Tāne, Tū, Tangaroa/Kanaloa, Rongo/Lono), the world-parents of Sky and Earth, the trickster-demigod Māui, and the twin concepts of mana and tapu. Māori tradition remains vividly living.
dossier.cosmos: In the Māori account cosmogony is genealogy: from Te Kore (the void) through the many nights of Te Pō to Te Ao Mārama (the world of light), the primal parents Ranginui (Sky) and Papatūānuku (Earth) lie locked in embrace until their children force them apart to admit the light. The atua then govern the domains — forest, sea, cultivation, war, storm. Humans descend from the gods; the first woman is shaped from earth, and death enters the world through the goddess Hine-nui-te-pō, to whose underworld (or to the ancestral homeland Hawaiki) the dead return.
dossier.practice: Life is regulated by tapu (sacred restriction) and by the winning and guarding of mana (efficacious sacred power); rites are conducted by tohunga (expert-priests) through karakia (chanted incantations), offerings, and first-fruits. Carved meeting-houses and marae among the Māori, and stone heiau temples in Hawaii, frame communal ritual, while houses of learning (whare wānanga) transmit the esoteric cosmological chants.
dossier.canon: Oral — genealogies (whakapapa) and cosmogonic and mythic chants recited by trained experts — first written down in the 19th century (notably Sir George Grey's collection and the whare-wānanga teachings recorded from figures such as Te Matorohanga).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the cosmos as genealogy — reality reckoned as descent from void to light to gods to people, so that to know the world is to be able to recite its lineage, and life is ordered by mana and tapu.
sources: Margaret Orbell, 'The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend' (1995)
Patrick V. Kirch & Roger C. Green, 'Hawaiki, Ancestral Polynesia: An Essay in Historical Anthropology' (2001)
